summary:
Outcast Fey are the class of Unseelie Fey whose bodies, names, or inheritances are stabilized by rejection. They are not simply ugly fey, malformed fey, exiles, criminals, changelings, mutants, or monsters that other courts dislike. An Outcast Fey belongs here when exclusion itself has become part of the creature’s fey biology: the rejected name, broken mask, denied lineage, half-accepted blood, failed court recognition, or impossible inheritance is what holds the creature together.

This class is the Unseelie home for fey-blooded misfits that bright courts cannot or will not classify cleanly: malformed fey heirs, courtless changeling descendants, mask-broken pages, rejected revel-children, fey with incompatible seasonal marks, abandoned title-bearers, and liminal creatures whose bodies prove a fey inheritance but whose form violates the expected pattern. It should not be used as a dumping ground for hags, fomorians, fiends, undead, aberrations, giants, or ordinary cursed mortals.

A field crew usually recognizes Outcast Fey by the tension between fey beauty and taxological refusal: mismatched wings, one hoof and one bare foot, antlers growing from only one side of the head, a voice that belongs to a courtly singer in a body no court mirror will hold, bark-like scar tissue over luminous veins, insect eyes in an elfin face, a shadow with a different silhouette, or a name that makes nearby fey wince.
tax:
Outcast Fey sit directly beneath Unseelie because rejection, exile, and failed recognition are recurring dark fey persistence mechanisms. Where Gloaming Court Fey are stabilized by title and audience, Outcast Fey are stabilized by the injury of denied title. Where Winter Fey are defined by cold law, Blood Fey by blood-price, Frightful Fey by fear, Spiteful Fey by provocation, and Dark Hunt Fey by quarry, Outcast Fey are defined by inheritance that has been refused but not erased.

The taxonomic test is not social status. A Seelie noble banished for treason does not automatically become Outcast Fey. A mortal child raised by fey does not qualify by upbringing alone. A malformed beast, cursed giant, hag-spawn, or fiend-touched creature does not belong here unless the body’s persistence is genuinely fey and the rejection has become a stable inherited or metaphysical condition.

Outcast Fey are also distinct from general Unseelie courtiers. A Gloaming Court Fey may be cruel, masked, and title-bound, but it belongs to a recognized court structure. An Outcast Fey may use court fragments — masks, titles, invitations, guest-rights, heraldry — yet those fragments are broken, stolen, denied, or reassembled around a wound in recognition. In practice, Outcast Fey often live near courts rather than inside them: under bridges outside palace roads, in bramble ghettos, beside abandoned revel rings, behind nursery mirrors, or in mortal border villages that do not know what they are sheltering.

A descendant belongs here only if outcasting is a biological, magical, or true-name condition. “Lives alone,” “is disliked,” “has a strange body,” or “was exiled from a court” is insufficient.
ap:
Outcast Fey vary more than any other Unseelie class because the class is defined by failure to match a sanctioned pattern. Their bodies often show two or more fey logics trying to occupy the same frame. A specimen may have moth wings and deer hooves, thorn antlers and fish-pale skin, childlike hands and an ancient face, one eye of summer gold and one of winter black, or a courtier’s posture in a body twisted by roots, chitin, feathers, or shadow.

The most reliable field sign is not deformity but incompatibility. The parts are not random. They are meaningful fey signs that should belong to different courts, seasons, bloodlines, or stories. A wing may shed spring pollen while the breath frosts glass. A hand may be delicate enough for harp strings while the nails are burrowing claws. A face may be beautiful only when reflected, while the living body seems unfinished. A voice may compel attention even when the mouth is sewn, hidden, or anatomically wrong.

Their glamour is usually unstable. It may flicker in mirrors, fail under a true name, cling too strongly to one feature, or produce the body the creature believes it should have rather than the body it has. Some wear masks, veils, hoods, patchwork finery, stolen court garments, broken crowns, or name-tags made from bone, bark, paper, or black ribbon. These coverings are not merely shame. They help the creature maintain a negotiable shape.

Spoor includes shed glamour like flakes of mica, footprints that change species halfway through a trail, mirror-fog that forms rejected names, discarded masks with no eyeholes, bloodless feathers, thorn splinters under nursery windows, and invitation cards torn exactly through the title line.
eco:
Outcast Fey persist in marginal habitats: bramble settlements outside courts, underbridges, abandoned revel grounds, ruined nurseries, changeling roads, half-collapsed underhill halls, old theatres after the audience is gone, border villages, refuse gardens behind fey palaces, roadside shrines to unnamed guests, and mortal houses that have unknowingly adopted a fey-blooded child or servant.

They eat ordinary food when embodied, often according to the strongest part of their inheritance: milk, fruit, meat, roots, honey, insects, mushrooms, blood, breath, attention, or dreams. Their deeper nourishment comes from recognition under pressure. Some feed on being named correctly despite rejection. Some stabilize through stolen titles. Some require masks, witnesses, fosterage, or dark-court shelter. Others persist through resentment, shame, secrecy, or the repeated refusal of a parent court to acknowledge them.

Reproduction is irregular. Some are born from fey bloodlines carrying incompatible inheritances. Some arise from changeling exchanges gone wrong. Some are lesser fey whose names were broken during failed promotion to a court office. Some are abandoned offspring of crossing seasonal, courtly, mortal, or monstrous lines. Some bud from social wounds: the uninvited child, the unrecognized heir, the servant who learned every dance but was never allowed a mask, the creature named “not ours” by a court with enough magical authority to make the phrase anatomical.

Outcast Fey populations may grow around courts that deny too much. A bright court obsessed with purity can cast a long Unseelie shadow. A dark court may shelter such beings, but shelter is not the same as healing. The Unseelie often gives them function: border guard, spy, living insult, hidden heir, nursery broker, omen-bearer, mask-keeper, or knife carried in a rejected name.
beh:
Outcast Fey are shaped by the experience of not fitting a rule that still controls them. Many are wary, observant, and intensely literate in etiquette, because they have survived by knowing exactly which door they are not allowed to enter. Some are shy mimics, collecting gestures, songs, clothes, and names from recognized fey in the hope that enough correct pieces will make them acceptable. Others become bitter, theatrical, or predatory, turning the shame of rejection outward.

They can be unusually sympathetic to mortals, changelings, exiles, bastards, or other border people, but sympathy should not be mistaken for safety. An Outcast Fey may protect a lost child because it remembers being refused — then keep that child forever because keeping is the only form of belonging it understands. Another may offer shelter to fugitives, only to demand that they surrender their old names so no one can reject them again.

Their relationship with courts is volatile. Gloaming Court Fey may use them as deniable agents. Seelie courts may pity, fear, deny, or erase them. Unseelie courts may welcome them with a title that is also a leash. Among other Unseelie, they often serve as translators between incompatible lineages because they understand partial belonging. They also make dangerous revolutionaries: no one studies a locked door more carefully than the creature forced to sleep outside it.

When threatened, Outcast Fey often attack the category rather than the body. They steal names, ruin heraldry, expose illegitimate heirs, break masks, invert guest-right, or force others to feel the instability they carry. Physical violence varies by descendant. Some are fragile and glamour-heavy; others are monstrous hybrids with claws, horns, venom, or impossible movement.
traitsText:
Type: Fey.

Senses: Low-light vision. Darkvision, scent, detect magic, see invisibility, dreamsense, or ancestry-specific senses may appear in descendant taxa, but should not be assumed across the whole class.

Languages: Sylvan is standard where the creature is intelligent enough for speech. Aklo, Common, Elven, Undercommon, Giant, regional court dialects, nursery speech, and broken title-cant are common in lower taxa. Some Outcast Fey speak in mixed registers: half courtly formula, half borrowed mortal idiom, with names avoided or overemphasized.

Proficiencies: As fey: simple weapons and any weapons named in the descendant entry. Outcast Fey may favor knives, pins, sickles, staves, claws, horns, teeth, stolen court blades, improvised tools, or no manufactured weapons. Armor proficiency applies only when the creature is described as wearing armor.

Defensive Traits: Common descendant defenses include DR/cold iron, unstable glamour, bonuses against polymorph or transmutation, resistance tied to a mask or name, partial immunity to courtly compulsion, or defensive abilities inherited from one visible bloodline. These must be specified locally; the class is too variable for a universal defensive package.

Weaknesses: Common weakness patterns include cold iron, true-name exposure, broken masks, public rejection, enforced recognition, court mirrors, title-stripping, restored birth-names, and rituals that separate incompatible inheritances. Some Outcast Fey are harmed by being denied; others are harmed by being correctly acknowledged. The lower taxon must define which pattern applies.

Skills: Bluff, Disguise, Escape Artist, Knowledge, Perception, Sense Motive, Sleight of Hand, Stealth, Survival, and Use Magic Device are common skill directions. Descendant taxa often receive racial bonuses to Disguise when masked, Stealth in marginal spaces, Sense Motive around court etiquette, or Knowledge checks concerning fey lineage and title.

Environment: Any fey borderland; commonly forests, ruins, underbridges, bramble margins, abandoned nurseries, changeling roads, underhill outskirts, mortal border villages, court refuse-gardens, and First World/Feywild thresholds.

Organization: Solitary, pair, foundling clutch, masked household, bramble band, rejected retinue, fostered nest, name-gang, or hidden court.
abilities:
Flickering Inheritance (Su). Outcast Fey often express unstable or incompatible fey ancestry through intermittent powers. A descendant may show partial glamour, strange movement, seasonal signs, venom, dream speech, thorn growth, charm, fear scent, or court-sense, but no one ability belongs to the whole class. Lower taxa should name the inherited feature, its source, its limit, and what causes it to flicker or fail.

Unstable Glamour (Su). An Outcast Fey’s glamour may conceal deformity, assemble a socially acceptable face, imitate a courtly body, or project the form the creature was denied. Unlike the refined glamour of Gloaming Court Fey, this glamour is prone to seams: mirrors show the wrong shape, shadows reveal missing or extra limbs, names cause the face to stutter, or cold iron pins the body into its least comfortable truth. Descendant entries may model this as disguise self, alter self-like effects, Disguise bonuses, miss chance, or involuntary revelation under defined triggers.

Rejected Mask (Su). Many Outcast Fey use a mask, veil, hood, ribbon, title-token, or garment to stabilize identity. While the item is worn, the fey may gain bonuses to Disguise, Will saves, court interaction, or access to a specific inherited power. If the mask is removed by rightful challenge, cold iron, public unmasking, or a true-name rite, the fey may lose those benefits, suffer penalties, or reveal a dangerous alternate form. The lower taxon should define the object and the removal condition.

Name of Refusal (Su). The phrase, title, or name by which a court rejected the fey can function as both wound and weapon. Spoken cruelly, it may impose penalties, suppress glamour, or trigger rage, fear, paralysis, or transformation. Reclaimed by the fey or spoken in protection, it may stabilize the creature, heal it, or break an enemy’s claim. This ability should be written with exact triggers and outcomes; random insults do not qualify.

Dark-Court Shelter (Su). Unseelie courts can stabilize Outcast Fey by granting a title, mask, border duty, insult-name, foster-right, or protected place. While under such shelter, a descendant may gain DR/cold iron, spell resistance, fast healing, social authority over other outcasts, or access to court roads. The benefit should come with a cost: obligation, service, inability to refuse summons, vulnerability to title-stripping, or dependence on the sheltering court.

Misfit Passage (Su). Outcast Fey may pass through spaces barred to more orderly fey because they belong to no single rule completely. They may slip through broken arches, rejected invitations, nursery thresholds, abandoned revel rings, half-ruined court gates, cracks in guest-law, or mortal homes that have unknowingly accepted them. This may be modeled as dimension door, pass without trace, tree stride, shadow step, or a custom threshold movement ability with strict liminal limits. It should not function as free teleportation.

Hybrid Sense (Ex or Su). Many Outcast Fey perceive one category their apparent body should not: sap-pulse through wood, fear-scent, iron pain, dream residue, court debt, changeling blood, hearth rejection, or the sound of an unused name. The sense must be specified locally and should not become a universal detection suite. It is often the clearest sign of their incompatible inheritance.

Court-Mirror Exposure (Su). Some Outcast Fey are revealed or harmed by mirrors, polished silver, still water, black glass, or other reflective objects used in court ritual. A descendant with this weakness may lose Disguise bonuses, become visible, suffer penalties, or be forced to confront its rejected form. The mirror must be ritually or socially meaningful; an ordinary puddle should not defeat the ability unless the lower taxon says so.

Borrowed Title (Su). An Outcast Fey may steal, borrow, counterfeit, or temporarily inhabit a minor title. While the title holds, it may command lesser fey, cross a court threshold, gain a skill bonus, or use a limited spell-like ability tied to the office. The title collapses if challenged by the rightful holder, disproven before witnesses, struck through with cold iron, or separated from the token that carries it.

Inheritance Flare (Su). Under stress — rejection, acceptance, injury, naming, masking, or court judgment — one suppressed inheritance may surge. A delicate foundling may grow thorns; a wingless page may unfold moth wings; a soft-spoken fosterling may speak with an ancestor’s geas; a one-hoofed child may run on moon roads. Descendant entries may express this as a temporary combat form, spell-like ability, natural attack, speed change, fear effect, or defensive boost. The trigger and duration must be explicit.

Recognition Bargain (Su). Some Outcast Fey can bargain around belonging. A creature that names them, shelters them, rejects them, adopts them, or witnesses them may become entangled in a supernatural obligation. This may create charm, geas-like duty, protective bond, curse, or shared vulnerability. It should never be unrestricted social control; the bargain must arise from an act of recognition or refusal and should include a release condition.
bg:
Outcast Fey matter because they make taxonomy politically dangerous. A ranger can misclassify a redcap and still know to fear the blood-wet cap. Misclassifying an Outcast Fey may create the very wound that empowers it. Calling it “monster,” “changeling,” “bastard,” “not fey,” or “not ours” may be more than rude. In the wrong place, those words are tools with edges.

Route-makers, Pall-Brokers, shrine-keepers, heralds, village elders, and court envoys record Outcast Fey carefully because the correct response depends on the creature’s rejected inheritance. Some must be named kindly. Some must never be granted a title. Some are safest when given guest-right but not adoption. Some must have their masks left untouched. Some can only be released by restoring a birth-name or witnessing the truth of a lineage that a court tried to erase.

This class also protects the catalogue from lazy placement. Fey-adjacent monsters often gather around Unseelie courts: hags advise them, fomorians war with or serve them, fiends bargain with them, undead haunt their roads, and mortal cults imitate their rites. Outcast Fey are not those neighbors. They are fey whose own inheritance has been damaged, denied, crossed, or refused so deeply that rejection became part of the body.
